aria_pop_traj_pre: Predator and prey population trajectories over
aria_phase_portrait_pre: Phase portrait of predator abundance versus prey abundance across
run_sim_env_data: Run the canvas simulation to see environment data.
route_sandbox_hint: Build a food web by hand.
title: Ecosystem Simulator
field_brief_lead: Pick a route, watch the live population system, and use the graph or campaign when you need evidence.
hero_explore_hint: Click any species to see what it eats and what eats it. Trophic-level cascades become obvious — remove a top predator and watch the system reorganize.
hero_sandbox_hint: Drop in producers, consumers, and predators; watch population dynamics emerge. Lotka-Volterra cycles appear when you have one predator + one prey + nothing else.
hero_conserve_hint: Explore a Maine-inspired 10-year teaching scenario. Population, habitat, and public-support values are 0-100 indices, and hand-authored cascade rules are not forecasts.
hero_inquiry_hint: Adjust predator birth, prey lifespan, and resource scarcity. Watch which discrete regime the system settles into. No score, no reveal, no answer dump — just slider sweep and observation.
hero_quiz_hint: Six questions cover predator-prey lag, model purpose, predation, carrying capacity, primary producers, and trophic levels.
hero_badges_hint: Achievements track which ecological concepts you have demonstrated, not just visited. Trophic-cascade badge requires you to actually trigger one in the sandbox.
explore_goal_body: compare settings that produce cycles, damping, or collapse in these teaching models. Predator peaks often lag prey peaks, but the amount of lag depends on the parameters. The animated canvas uses separate stochastic rules; the graph below uses a deterministic logistic predator-prey equation.
aria_live_pop_hist_pre: Live predator and prey population history over
canvas_generating_data: Canvas simulation is generating live data...
explore_model_desc: Explore a logistic extension of the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model. Adjust initial populations and coefficients to compare cycles, density dependence, and extinction in a discrete Euler approximation.
teaching_model_note: Teaching model: one-step Euler updates with rounded counts. It omits age structure, space, seasons, stochasticity, handling time, disease, and changing K; parameter values are illustrative rather than fitted to rabbits and foxes.
sandbox_goal_body: design experiments. Try the textbook Lotka-Volterra setup (1 predator + 1 prey, nothing else) and watch the cycle. Then break it: add a second predator (competitive exclusion), or remove the predator entirely (prey overshoots carrying capacity and crashes). Inject events to test resilience. Every entity placed is graded by Carrying Capacity (K) on the slider below.
aria_sandbox_prefix: Ecosystem sandbox. Click to place prey (left) or predators (right).
select_tool_intro: Select a tool above, then click on the canvas to interact:
instr_place: Place Rabbit/Fox/Tree: Click anywhere to spawn
instr_erase: Erase: Click near an entity to remove it
instr_move: Move: Click and drag an entity to reposition it
instr_tip: Tip: Pause the simulation first for precise placement!
exp_idea_1: Remove all predators and watch what happens to prey and vegetation
exp_idea_2: Create a "wall" of trees and see if it affects hunting patterns
exp_idea_3: Add many foxes at once, then trigger a Food Boom to save the rabbits
exp_idea_4: Pause, place prey in one corner and predators in the other, then resume
exp_idea_5: Trigger a Wildfire then immediately add trees to observe recovery
tip_wolf_priority: Build toward wolf reintroduction AND raise deer quota
tip_wolf_text_post: index) and wolves are absent. Without predators, deer degrade every habitat through browse pressure. Two parallel moves: raise the deer hunting quota for immediate relief, and start the long arc toward wolf reintroduction (Compensation fund + Public education to lift wolf support above 50, plus Habitat protection to lift wolf habitat above 60).
tip_salmon_priority: Stream restoration for salmon and trout
tip_salmon_text_post: ). Stream restoration helps salmon, brook trout, and beaver in one move. Dam removal is a bigger lever but costs public support.
tip_hold_priority: Hold steady and read the land
tip_hold_text: No single zone is in crisis. Use Year 1 to monitor and protect habitat; the cascade rules will compound your moves in later years.
ai_no_text_fallback: The reader returned no text. Try again in a moment.
ai_offline: The AI reader is offline right now. Try again in a moment.
ai_unavailable: AI is not available in this context.
ai_reading_status: AI conservation biologist is reading your ecosystem...
ai_disclaimer2: It is not a Wabanaki person, not a wildlife professional, and does not speak for any Wabanaki nation, agency, or organization.
ai_disclaimer3: For authoritative voices on Maine conservation work, consult Penobscot Cultural and Historic Preservation Department, Passamaquoddy Cultural Heritage Museum, Wabanaki Public Health and Wellness, Maine Indian Basketmakers Alliance, Maine Department of Inland Fisheries and Wildlife, and the Atlantic Salmon Federation.
foodweb_cascade_caption: Food web: cascade rules active in real time
aria_foodweb_diagram: Food-web diagram of the 6 Maine species
what_you_can_do: What you can do for this species in the sim
aria_trend_chart: Population-index trend chart by species year-by-year
event_quiet_year_desc: No major event. Routine fieldwork, steady progress.
outcome_mastery_desc: You held the whole web together. Wolves are back. Beavers are reshaping streams. Salmon are running. Brook trout hold the headwaters. This is what landscape-scale recovery looks like.
outcome_skilled_desc: You met most of the recovery targets. Some species are thriving, others are still climbing. The trajectory is good.
outcome_mixed_desc: A few species made meaningful gains. Others stalled or slipped. Conservation is rarely clean.
outcome_losing_desc: The ecosystem is slipping. Populations are low, habitat is degraded, public support is fragile. This is how species disappear quietly.
steward_intro: Steward six species across 10 years of fieldwork.
setup_intro_1: You are the lead manager in a Maine-inspired teaching scenario. Six species. Ten modeled years. The catch:
setup_intro_2: The scenario links wolves, deer browse, beaver wetlands, migratory fish, and stream habitat through hand-authored rules. Population, habitat, and support values are relative indices, not animal counts; outcomes are not forecasts or management recommendations.
what_if_nothing: What if you had done nothing for 10 years?
neglect_outperformed: Pure neglect outperformed active management this run. Trophic cascade went poorly. Look at which interventions cost you ground.
moved_ahead: You moved the ecosystem substantially ahead of where neglect would have left it. The gap is the conservation infrastructure you built.
held_the_line: You roughly held the line against drift. Conservation that matches the do-nothing baseline still counts: stasis is the holding ground for everything you build later.
aria_ask_ai_read: Ask AI conservation biologist to read your ecosystem state
title_ai_reads_state: AI conservation educator reads your current state
no_actions_yet: No actions yet this year. Pick a species, pick a technique.
iq_boom_desc: In this teaching rule, the predator-birth input outweighs prey-lifespan and scarcity terms.
iq_balanced_desc: Neither arbitrary score crosses the classifier threshold; this does not demonstrate ecological equilibrium.
iq_collapse_desc: In this teaching rule, the scarcity score crosses its classifier threshold.
iq_intro: Sweep three conceptual sliders and compare an arbitrary weighted classifier. The labels are prompts for inquiry, not Lotka-Volterra solutions, population forecasts, or measured ecological rates.
hypothesis_label: Your hypothesis (free text — no right answer):
iq_hypo_placeholder: Which slider matters MOST for triggering a collapse? Can you balance the system with predator birth at 80%? Type your own theory.
iq_stuck_btn: I'm stuck — show me questions to think about (no answers)
iq_q1: Start with all three at 50%. What outcome do you get? Now raise predator birth slowly. At what point does the regime flip?
iq_q2: Set predator birth high (80%). Can you save the system by changing ONLY prey lifespan? What if you change only resource scarcity?
iq_q3: Try to find TWO different settings that both produce a balanced cycle. What do they have in common?
iq_q4: Log 4-5 observations of each regime (boom, balanced, collapse). Look for patterns in the table — are there single-variable thresholds, or do they interact?
iq_q5: Ecological transfer efficiency varies among systems; about 10% is only a rough teaching heuristic. What additional information would you need to connect energy flow to predator abundance?
no_answers_revealed: No answers will be revealed. Investigate.
iq_understand_label: I think I understand the trade-offs — let me explain them in my own words
iq_explain_placeholder: Explain in your own words: how do predator birth, prey lifespan, and resource scarcity interact? Why does balanced cycling require something specific? When do predators win? When does the system collapse?
iq_saved_note: Saved. Notice — nobody checked your answer. That is what learner-driven inquiry looks like.
iq_model_limit: Model limit: two arbitrary weighted scores create three regime labels. This inquiry widget is not a population-dynamics model, does not calculate trophic energy transfer, and should not be interpreted as a forecast.
quiz_covers_intro: 6 multiple-choice questions on foundational ecology concepts:
quiz_topic_1: Predator response after prey availability increases
quiz_topic_2: What the Lotka-Volterra family of models represents
quiz_topic_6: Trophic levels in food chains and webs
quiz_covers_footer: Each question links back to behavior you can produce in Explore or Sandbox. If a concept feels abstract, swap to Sandbox and rebuild the scenario.
answer_to_track: Answer questions to track your progress
tut_step_1: Welcome to the Ecosystem Simulator! Watch rabbits and foxes interact in the canvas above.
tut_step_2: Choose a biome at the top to change the environment. Each has unique colors and ecology.
tut_step_3: Trigger environmental events to test the ecosystem’s resilience! Try Drought or Wildfire.
tut_step_4: Complete ecology challenges by maintaining specific population patterns. Earn RP!
tut_step_5: Run the graph simulation with Lotka–Volterra equations. Try all 4 presets!
